metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the Testing section (or AC2), add concrete test function names (e.g. `test_project_default_dig`, `test_query_project_raises_when_unset`, `test_assert_env_ready_names_missing_vars`, `test_apply_handlers_catches_neutral_assignee_error`) so 'verified by the unit tests named in Testing' is a deterministic reference.
evidence: 'verified by the unit tests named in Testing' — AC2 body
Testing section names three scenario groups but zero test function names
'Happy path: JiraBackend.project returns the DIG-defaulted project; query_project returns the configured project; assert_env_ready() passes with all three JIRA_* set' — no test_* identifier given
findings.finding: AC2's proving command is stated as 'verified by the unit tests named in Testing', but the Testing section describes behavioral scenarios (happy path, failure/edge, gate) without naming any specific test functions (e.g. test_project_default, test_query_project_fail_closed). An executor cannot deterministically construct a `pytest -k` filter or confirm coverage without inferring test names.
scenarios: An executor implementing the plan cannot confirm AC2 is met without reading test output holistically; a reviewer cannot verify specific behavioral coverage from the plan alone.
A future regression in query_project fail-closed behavior may not be caught if the test for it was never explicitly named or linked.
findings.suggested_fix: Append a bullet to the Testing section listing proposed test names, e.g.: 'Gate tests: `test_no_vendor_imports_full_tree` (ast.walk); Contract tests: `test_jira_backend_project_default`, `test_jira_backend_query_project_fail_closed`, `test_assert_env_ready_raises_backend_env_error`, `test_apply_handlers_soft_fail_on_backend_assignee_not_found`.' This makes AC2's proving command anchored and reviewable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `BackendEnvError` to the `_backend.py` scope annotation so Scope, Approach, and AC2 all list the same complete set of additions to that file.
evidence: Scope: '(port: project/query_project/assert_env_ready + BackendAssigneeNotFoundError)' — BackendEnvError absent
Approach: 'raises a new neutral BackendEnvError(RuntimeError) (also defined in `_backend.py`)'
AC2: 'raising BackendEnvError'
findings.finding: The Scope annotation for `_backend.py` lists '(port: project/query_project/assert_env_ready + BackendAssigneeNotFoundError)' but omits `BackendEnvError`, which the Approach explicitly places in that file ('also defined in `_backend.py`') and AC2 references as the raised type from `assert_env_ready()`. An implementer reading Scope as the authoritative file-change manifest would miss that `BackendEnvError` must be added to `_backend.py`.
findings.location: Scope section — `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_backend.py` annotation; cross-referenced with Approach paragraph 3 and AC2
scenarios: An implementer treats the Scope parenthetical as the complete list of additions to `_backend.py`, adds `BackendAssigneeNotFoundError` but not `BackendEnvError`, and the typecheck fails on the `assert_env_ready()` return type annotation — a subtle miss caught only at CI time.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the Scope line to: `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_backend.py (port: project/query_project/assert_env_ready + BackendEnvError + BackendAssigneeNotFoundError)`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split AC2 into at least two focused criteria: one for the Protocol/JiraBackend/FakeBackend/S2 structural changes (typecheck-verifiable) and one for behavioral parity of the four consumer rewirings (unit-test-verifiable), so each has a single observable outcome and a single proving command.
evidence: 'The Backend port exposes project, query_project, assert_env_ready() (raising BackendEnvError), and BackendAssigneeNotFoundError; JiraBackend implements them by delegating to resolve_jira_settings; the S2 contract suite and FakeBackend are updated to the widened Protocol; behavior is identical (DIG default in the applier guard, fail-closed fetcher JQL scoping, _attestation JIRA_* fail-fast, assignee soft-fail) — verified by the unit tests named in Testing.'
Single checkbox covers Protocol, JiraBackend, S2 suite, FakeBackend, and four behavioral claims
findings.finding: AC2 is a compound criterion that bundles five distinct deliverables into a single checkbox: (1) Backend Protocol widened with three new members + two new exceptions, (2) JiraBackend implements them, (3) S2 contract suite updated, (4) FakeBackend updated, and (5) behavioral parity for four separate consumer behaviors. Each sub-item has its own failure mode, making it difficult to declare the AC done or partially done during implementation.
scenarios: An agent completing the Protocol extension but not yet the FakeBackend update cannot mark AC2 green, obscuring partial progress.
A reviewer cannot assess which sub-deliverable a failing test maps to without decomposing the AC manually.

findings.finding: The plan asserts the new AST case in test_backend_neutrality.py will catch 'module-level or function-nested' adapters.jira imports, but the existing _module_level_imported_names helper only walks tree.body (top-level AST), and ALL FOUR of the vendor-import sites being removed (fetcher.py:379/441, applier.py:603, _attestation.py:65) are function-nested lazy imports — so the existing helper infrastructure cannot catch a regression in the function-nested positions the gate is supposed to pin.
findings.location: AC1: 'NO adapters.jira/acli_subprocess import (module-level or function-nested) — verified by the new AST case in tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_neutrality.py'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the body of the new gate test with an ast.walk scan: `all_from_imports = [n for n in ast.walk(tree) if isinstance(n, ast.ImportFrom)]`, then check that no node's module string contains 'adapters.jira' or 'acli_subprocess'. This covers both module-level and function-nested positions as AC1 requires.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the _backend.py 'no behavior / stdlib + typing only' docstring constraint in the plan: either update the constraint to allow exception base classes, or explicitly justify why BackendEnvError and BackendAssigneeNotFoundError belong there rather than in the existing `_errors.py` module.
evidence: _backend.py lines 6-7: 'This module defines the vendor-neutral interface ... It is *pure interface*: ``typing.Protocol`` declarations plus the ``RemoteRef`` identity value — no behavior, no vendor imports, stdlib + typing only'
Plan states: 'A neutral BackendAssigneeNotFoundError exception defined in _backend.py' and 'BackendEnvError(RuntimeError) (also defined in _backend.py)'
RuntimeError subclasses are concrete behavior-carrying classes, contradicting the 'no behavior' invariant the file's docstring asserts
findings.finding: The plan places BackendEnvError(RuntimeError) and BackendAssigneeNotFoundError as concrete exception classes in _backend.py, but the existing _backend.py module docstring explicitly declares the file to be 'pure interface: Protocol declarations plus the RemoteRef identity value — no behavior, no vendor imports, stdlib + typing only.' Adding concrete exception classes with behavior contradicts this stated invariant, which the plan asserts as a property of the file.
findings.location: Approach section ('A neutral BackendAssigneeNotFoundError exception defined in _backend.py') and Scope ('src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_backend.py (port: ... + BackendAssigneeNotFoundError)')
scenarios: An implementor reading the docstring would not expect concrete exception classes to live in _backend.py and might place them in _errors.py instead, causing an import-site mismatch.

The 'no behavior' invariant is also load-path relevant: the docstring says the file 'loads in every context the reconciler is exec_d in (normal import and spec_from_file_location by-path)'; exception class definitions don't break this, but the doc constraint signals intent that future reviewers may enforce.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the Approach section: '_backend.py's docstring will be updated to reflect that neutral exception base classes (BackendEnvError, BackendAssigneeNotFoundError) are permitted alongside Protocol declarations, since they carry no vendor logic and are pure interface contracts.' Alternatively, place the exceptions in _errors.py and import them into _backend.py's public surface — but confirm the import does not violate the 'no vendor imports' constraint.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `assert_env_ready()` has ≥2 additional concrete call sites (beyond `_attestation.py`) in this ticket's scope, or demote it to a `SupportsEnvReadiness` opt-in capability Protocol so only backends that have meaningful env checks implement it.
evidence: Plan: 'assert_env_ready() — raises a new neutral BackendEnvError… JiraBackend carries the current _attestation.py:72-79 JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER/JIRA_API_TOKEN check'
No second or third call site of `backend.assert_env_ready()` is named anywhere in the plan, Testing section, or Scope list
The plan itself says 'every backend has *some* scoping/readiness story' — a hypothetical future backend, not a current done-definition

Existing pattern: `inbound_probe.py:29` defines `ProbeConfigError(RuntimeError)` doing the identical job at module scope, showing the codebase already has a working pattern for this concern without a Protocol method
findings.finding: `assert_env_ready()` is placed as a required member on the `Backend` Protocol but has exactly ONE documented call site (`_attestation.py:72-79`), failing Rule-of-Three. The plan's justification ('every backend has *some* readiness story') is a hypothetical rather than a current done-definition.
findings.location: Approach section — 'assert_env_ready()' bullet; AC line 2; _backend.py scope entry
scenarios: FakeBackend must implement `assert_env_ready()` to satisfy structural typing, requiring a stub that has no natural meaning for an in-memory test double
Any future non-Jira backend must implement `assert_env_ready()` even if its env-readiness check is trivial or nonexistent

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `BackendEnvError` and `ProbeConfigError` can share a common base (e.g. `BackendEnvError` added to `_errors.py` which `inbound_probe.ProbeConfigError` subclasses), or document in the ADR why the spec-loader isolation makes unification impossible.
evidence: `inbound_probe.py:29`: `class ProbeConfigError(RuntimeError): 'Raised when required env vars are missing.'`
`_attestation.py:84`: `raise RuntimeError(f'missing JIRA_* configuration: {…}')` — the exact pattern `BackendEnvError` replaces
Plan introduces `BackendEnvError(RuntimeError)` defined in `_backend.py` for the same semantic purpose

The spec-loader isolation of `ProbeConfigError` is a real constraint (it cannot be imported from `_backend.py`), but `BackendEnvError` and `ProbeConfigError` will coexist as parallel 'missing env vars' exceptions with no relationship
findings.finding: `BackendEnvError` rebuilds the same semantics as the already-existing `ProbeConfigError(RuntimeError)` in `inbound_probe.py` (both are 'raised when required env vars are missing'), introducing a parallel exception type for the same concern.
scenarios: A caller catching env-readiness errors must know whether to catch `BackendEnvError`, `ProbeConfigError`, or `RuntimeError` depending on which code path raised — no unified catch point despite two parallel types
findings.suggested_fix: If `_errors.py` is accessible at module-load time in the spec-loader context (it is already imported by `acli_subprocess.py`), define `BackendEnvError` in `_errors.py` and have `ProbeConfigError` subclass it, creating a single catch point.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add `outbound_assignee.py` to scope (migrating its `type(exc).__name__ == 'AssigneeNotFoundError'` duck-type check to `isinstance(exc, BackendAssigneeNotFoundError)`) or add an explicit explanatory note in the plan for why the existing name-based workaround is intentionally retained post-ticket.
evidence: outbound_assignee.py:113: if type(exc).__name__ == 'AssigneeNotFoundError': — explicit duck-typed workaround to avoid the vendor import
Plan Scope section lists only fetcher.py, applier.py, apply_handlers.py, _attestation.py for rewiring; outbound_assignee.py is absent
Plan introduces BackendAssigneeNotFoundError in _backend.py — the neutral base class outbound_assignee.py could now import without violating ADR 0035
findings.finding: outbound_assignee.py uses a deliberate duck-typed workaround (type(exc).__name__ == 'AssigneeNotFoundError' at line 113) to avoid importing AssigneeNotFoundError from the vendor layer. This module is a functional consumer of the AssigneeNotFoundError semantic contract but is not enumerated in scope, and the plan does not explain why it is excluded or whether it should migrate to isinstance(exc, BackendAssigneeNotFoundError) once the neutral base class exists.
scenarios: A reviewer or implementer lands the new BackendAssigneeNotFoundError but outbound_assignee.py's duck-typed check silently stays — the module never migrates to the clean neutral base, leaving the pattern fragmented across two styles.
If the exception class is ever renamed (or the name-check string drifts), outbound_assignee.py silently stops catching the error — a latent correctness bug the plan's new AST gate does not cover.
findings.suggested_fix: Add outbound_assignee.py to the scope file list; replace `if type(exc).__name__ == 'AssigneeNotFoundError':` with `from rebar_reconciler._backend import BackendAssigneeNotFoundError` (lazy, inside the function) and `if isinstance(exc, BackendAssigneeNotFoundError):`.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
